operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Contributing to OpenCog (14f758468a0d12a0)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Contributing to OpenCog
Messages: 2
Date range: 28 Aug 2015 to 2015-08-28
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f758468a0d12a0

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Elita Lobo
Date: 28 Aug 2015

Hi All, I am Elita Lobo, 4th year ECE student of NIT Durgapur. I am interested in contributing to any OpenCog project involving machine learning. I have experience with C++, Python, Go and Machine Learning.

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-08-28

Hi Elita, It is best if you have a clear idea of what you would want to do with OpenCog. The best way to learn is to start fixing bugs listed in github. After that, you might want to implement one of the ideas on the wiki: http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Ideas

--linas

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Contributing to OpenCog (15013a1de089933f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Contributing to OpenCog
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-09-28 to 2015-09-30
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 15013a1de089933f

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: Vatika Harlalka
Date: 2015-09-28

I am a 3rd year CS student and would like to contribute to OpenCog. I went through the issues on github but I am confused on which ones I should work on being a beginner.

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-09-28

There is something that would be incredibly useful: fix one of OpenCog's failing unit tests. If you've compiled the unit tests you might have noticed not all of them pass. You may pick any one and try to figure out why it fails. Those failures prevent us from really using our nice circleci automatic unit test checker.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Vatika Harlalka
Date: 2015-09-29

So, the anaphora test is in opencog/tests/nlp/anaphora/test_anaphora.py. Even after commenting all the test_* functions, the test is shown as failed.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-29

The test passes, for me, when I run it manually, when I set up the python library search paths correctly. This implies that there are some kind of python configuration issues when one runs make test.

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Vatika Harlalka
Date: 2015-09-30

make test tracks down to CTestTestfile.cmake. If the tests run independently then there must be an issue with the PYTHONPATH paths?

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-09-30

Well, if you read the README file in the tests/cython directory it will tell you how to run the tests by hand. If you do that, do they pass? If so, then what is different between the unit test, and what you do by hand?

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Contributing to OpenCog (150b357c75b814cf)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Contributing to OpenCog

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Dhruvin Patel
Date: 29 Oct 2015

Hi, I am Senior Undergraduate Computer Science Student. I am deeply interested in AI and Machine Learning and hence wish to contribute to ML projects of OpenCog.

--- Message 2/3 ---

Hi, welcome to the community :-) you can checkout http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/Meta-Optimizing_Semantic_Evolutionary_Search for an overview, check MOSES_for_Noobs for tutorials and the examples directory for more examples.

--- Message 3/3 ---

Thanks!

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Contributing to OpenCog (150f3d2a690792ac)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Contributing to OpenCog
Messages: 3
Date range: 10 Nov 2015 to 2015-11-12
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150f3d2a690792ac

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Ehab Albadawy
Date: 10 Nov 2015

Hi Guys, My name ehab albadawy, I want to contribute to one of opencog projects, specifically computer vision ones.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-11-11

Hi, Our machine-vision component used to be Destin, but last I talked to Ben, he seemed to have soured on it.

We currently do some ad-hoc work, using off-the-shelf software to detect human faces in a video stream for a robot demo w/ Hanson Robotics.

--linas

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-12

I had been thinking about integrating deep learning based vision into OpenCog according to these ideas:
http://blog.opencog.org/2015/11/01/putting-deep-perceptual-learning-in-opencog/

but now am thinking tensorflow may be a better choice than Theano...

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Cosine Distance of words (14f2ab431160989c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Cosine Distance of words
Messages: 7
Date range: 2015-08-14 to 2015-10-17
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14f2ab431160989c

============================================================

--- Message 1/7 ---
From: Rohit Shinde
Date: 2015-08-14

I have been writing code for computing cosine distance between two words. How do I compute the cosine score? Do I see if their decoded disjuncts exactly match?

--- Message 2/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-02

Yes. If a connector is missing, it is the same as being present with a count of zero. For example, bicycle has disjuncts (the- and is+) or (a- and was+), and car has (a- and was+) and (the- and ran+). The cosine similarity computes the sum of products of matching disjunct probabilities. Missing disjuncts count as zero.

--- Message 3/7 ---
From: Rohit Shinde
Date: 2015-10-11

Okay I get this now. Take all unique decoded disjuncts among a pair and calculate their cosine distance.

--- Message 4/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-11

That would be good. Extraction from what? There are no perl bindings to the atomspace, so I am confused.

--- Message 5/7 ---
From: Rohit Shinde
Date: 2015-10-12

You had told me to write a script to automate disjunct extraction from sentences from wikipedia articles.

--- Message 6/7 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-12

The goal is always to maintain statistics as a result of parsing. One needs to obtain the disjuncts to compute the cosine-distance metric. The cosine-distance metric will be used to create clusters. Clustering takes a set of word-disjunct pairs, and converts them into a single (set-of-words)-disjunct pair. The idea is that the set-of-words resembles a part-of-speech.

--- Message 7/7 ---
From: Rohit Shinde
Date: 2015-10-17

Yes, that is exactly what I am saying. We need to obtain disjuncts. I had to write a script and I am debugging it.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Culturia implementation description (ref PredicateLink Proposal) (15023a9412a3073c)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Culturia implementation description (ref PredicateLink Proposal)
Messages: 4
Date range: 2015-10-01 to 2015-10-05
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 15023a9412a3073c

============================================================

--- Message 1/4 ---
From: Amirouche Boubekki
Date: 2015-10-01

This is mildly related to the discussion about PredicateLink. I experimented with hypergraph databases and wanted to see how atomspace schema compares to graphdb.

--- Message 2/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-02

For all practical purposes, the atomspace just stores graphs, and the word hypergraph does nothing but confuse. Actually, the atomspace stores forests which are collections of trees. It does NOT really store graphs in general.

--- Message 3/4 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-03

The pattern matcher can handle simple and complex PLN searches. The question is how to do this efficiently. Its NP-complete in general; the atomspace has some support for indexing, but this is still very much a work in progress.

--- Message 4/4 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller via opencog
Date: 2015-10-05

Note that you probably want to have the entity as member, not the inheritance link. The PM query for that would use an AndLink with PredicateLink and InheritanceLink.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Deer Conscious Cognition Project Unity, Opencog, ZeroMQ (1507239a05c17b7f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Deer Conscious Cognition Project Unity, Opencog, ZeroMQ
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-10-16 to 2015-10-22
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1507239a05c17b7f

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 2015-10-16

I have completed the first prototype of my first non-toy Opencog project. The main purpose was to get basic plumbing in place to connect Unity3D and Opencog via ZeroMQ. I achieved very close to real time synchronization on a 40hz frame rate simulation, pushing about 200,000 messages per second. [...]

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-10-16

Thanks Jim -- Along with many other purposes, this may be useful as a playground for ECAN tweaking...

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-10-20

Wow, it is really cool to see you are moving forward with that project, Jim! Have you put the code online?

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Matt Chapman
Date: 2015-10-20

Really cool! Thanks for sharing. I am hoping to read your spec in full when I have time.

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Jim Rutt
Date: 2015-10-22

I have uploaded the software for AIDeer as three github code repositories at github.com/memetic007. Warning: this code is rough prototype style code. I have long preferred building a throw away first prototype to achieve end-to-end connectivity and benchmark the couplings for performance.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Defining default stv for r2l output (150b76f68ef9d94e)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Defining default stv for r2l output
Messages: 2
Date range: 29 Oct 2015 to 30 Oct 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150b76f68ef9d94e

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Tareq Alkhaldi
Date: 29 Oct 2015

Hi all, I've passed by this issue https://github.com/opencog/opencog/issues/781 and I'm wondering how is this applied in the new ure based r2l because PLN formulas break when dealing with the current default tv (1 0).

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 30 Oct 2015

Yes, a new default stv for R2L output was added in the past. However, at some point there was a decision to use ptv instead, to keep track the count of how many times a word is used. Then it was discovered the old default stv setting method kept overwriting the count.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+DeSTIN updates (14ac80fb087f1f0a)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: DeSTIN updates
Messages: 3
Date range: 7 Jan 2015 to 2015-01-07
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14ac80fb087f1f0a

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Kevin Sebastian
Date: 7 Jan 2015

Hello all, I just wanted to know updates on the DeSTIN project since summer.

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-07

Hi Kevin, Honestly not a lot is happening with DeSTIN now. Tewodros made a python version but it doesn't yet have all the features of the C version. I am still very interested in DeSTIN but don't have a DeSTIN-appropriate funding source right now.

ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-07

I became convinced the C codebase was too messy, and decided we should try moving to a python version based on Theano. It seems the unique value we can add in the deep learning vision space has to do with exporting patterns from DeSTIN into the Atomspace, doing reasoning there, and sending feedback back to DeSTIN.

-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Distributional TV (150d7628746f5232)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Distributional TV
Messages: 7
Date range: 2015-11-05 to 2015-11-06
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 150d7628746f5232

============================================================

--- Message 1/7 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-05

We ultimately need distributional TV. The naive approach of using simple TV for ImplicationLink fails: if P has confidence 0 and Q has confidence 1, the implication should be almost surely true, but naive formula gives confidence 0. To overcome this one needs distributional TVs. I need distributional TVs today so I can remove all hacks from the AGI-15 demo.

--- Message 2/7 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-05

Maybe one can derive a good and cheap formula using indefinite TV instead of simple TV in that case... So far we have only ever used simple TV and simple count TV.

--- Message 3/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-06

In general, if A with TV type T1 and B with TV type T2 are combined using an inference rule R to yield C, the rule is to project both T1 and T2 into the simplest form of TV for which there is a formula corresponding to rule R. We had indefinite TV in a version of Novamente Cognition engine, some code for it is still around, but it is very slow.

--- Message 4/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-06

After some reflection, I concluded it would be better perhaps to skip indefinite TV and just go straight for distributional TV. This is because for applications involving numerical data we actually want distributions not just intervals. The simplest way is to consider step function distributions discretized into say, 10 bins. For the deduction rule, the independence-based TV formula here is just matrix multiplication.

--- Message 5/7 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-06

So if T1 is simple and T2 is indefinite, and R has 2 formulae, it would project T2 to simple TV and use the simple TV formula.

--- Message 6/7 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-11-06

Got it. Yes, it feels to me going straight for distributional TV might just be more fruitful.

--- Message 7/7 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-11-06

Yeah. You got my suggestion...

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Draft designs for Perception Synthesis, Action Generation (14e9a54846e57a63)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Draft designs for Perception Synthesis, Action Generation

============================================================

--- Message 1/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-07-17

Here are draft designs for two OpenCog components, written mainly with

robotics applications in mind (but also to potentially be useful for

video-game and other applications):



Perception synthesis:



http://wiki.hansonrobotics.com/w/Perception_synthesizer



Action generation:



http://wiki.hansonrobotics.com/w/High-Level_Design_for_Action_Generation



linked from



http://wiki.hansonrobotics.com/w/Action_orchestrator





...



some of the particulars are fairly tuned to the Hanson Robotics

application, but the overall architecture/dynamics proposals are

intended to be of general applicability...



-- Ben

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Errata PLN book (151698c5a77bd447)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Errata PLN book
Messages: 6
Date range: 2015-12-03 to 2015-12-03
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 151698c5a77bd447

============================================================

--- Message 1/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-03

I think I found an error in the PLN book. First page of Chapter 6: the probability mass should be (1-b_i)/2 instead of b_i/2.

--- Message 2/6 ---
From: Matt Ikle
Date: 2015-12-03

I think that the b_i/2 are the tails and that 1-b_i is the center of the distribution. I.E. b=.05 would mean each tail would have area .025.

--- Message 3/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

b is the frequency of possible worlds in which the observed frequency will lie in the interval [L, U]. So, it would be b=.95 typically, not b=.05. Wow, I haven't thought about this in eons... cool to see it getting used though.

--- Message 4/6 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-12-03

Nil, I think you are right... it seems when Matt wrote that he may have gotten temporarily confused as to whether we were using b=.95 or b=.05 as the convention...

--- Message 5/6 ---
From: Matthew Ikle
Date: 2015-12-03

Looking over the page now, I see the issue. Nil's interpretation works correctly and would be the easiest fix. Though I was probably trying to say the same thing using b = 0.05 but failed.

--- Message 6/6 ---
From: 'Nil Geisweiller' via opencog
Date: 2015-12-03

Fixed on the wiki page, thanks.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Error while using octool to build opencog in Ubuntu 14.04 (14bfd3ce30877ef0)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Error while using octool to build opencog in Ubuntu 14.04
Messages: 3
Date range: 8 Mar 2015 to 9 Mar 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14bfd3ce30877ef0

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Chitrank Dixit
Date: 8 Mar 2015

Hello OpenCog developers, I am trying to install OpenCog using octool. When I run ./octool -b I get: CMake Error: Invalid line in atom_types.script file: [MST_Connector <- LINK]

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 9 Mar 2015

A recent pull request broke the build. Hopefully it will be fixed soon. In the mean time, you can edit the atom_types.script and capitalize MST_CONNECTOR to fix locally.

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 9 Mar 2015

Hi, I see your fork is a few commits behind, so it can't be the recent commit. If you are using a virtual machine make sure you have at least 2GB memory and follow steps at http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Building_OpenCog_on_Ubuntu
